worth_it_explanation: Reporting is the number a publisher runs the business on, so a gap between what OpenX reports and what actually pays out turns into forecasts that miss, reconciliations that do not tie out, and decisions made on figures that were never right. The check is a review of reports and remittances the publisher already has, so it costs reading and matching time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete discrepancy the publisher can trace to its source.
intro: Reporting accuracy is whether the revenue and fill a publisher reads inside OpenX matches what the inventory actually earned and what the payment eventually settles at. OpenX runs a Prebid-compatible header bidding wrapper, so a publisher often reads the same auction from more than one place, the OpenX console, a header bidding analytics adapter, and the ad server, and those places do not always agree. These checks confirm that reported revenue and fill reconcile against the remittance the publisher is actually paid, and that report dimensions and timezones are configured so the numbers compare cleanly across the systems a publisher reads them in.

audit_checks.title: Reported revenue and fill are not reconciled against actual payment
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Reported revenue inside OpenX is an estimate of what an auction earned, and the remittance a publisher is actually paid can differ from it because of discrepancies, deductions, invalid traffic adjustments, and revenue share. A publisher that never reconciles the two reads an estimate as if it were settled income, so a persistent shortfall between reported and paid revenue can run for months before anyone notices, which is revenue leakage at the scale of the whole OpenX line.

educate.base:
The revenue a publisher reads inside an OpenX report is an estimate of what the inventory earned in the auction. The money that lands in the bank is the remittance, the amount OpenX actually pays after the auction settles, and the two are not the same number. The remittance reflects deductions the report does not always show at the moment it is read, including the revenue share OpenX keeps, adjustments for invalid traffic that demand-side partners claw back, discrepancies between what a buyer reports and what the seller reports, and any spend that fails to clear. Reconciliation is the act of matching the reported figure against the remittance so a publisher knows the gap and where it comes from.

Without that match, a publisher runs the business on the estimate. Forecasts get built on reported revenue, board numbers get quoted from it, and a content or inventory decision gets justified by it, all before the money has settled. When the remittance arrives lower, the shortfall is hard to trace after the fact because the auctions that produced it are weeks old. A small, steady gap between reported and paid revenue is the kind of thing that hides in plain sight because each month looks roughly right on its own and only the running total shows the leak.

OpenX is a Prebid-compatible header bidding wrapper, which adds a second reason the numbers can drift. The same impression is often counted in more than one place, the OpenX console, a Prebid analytics adapter, and the ad server, and each measures at a slightly different point in the auction. A publisher reconciling revenue needs to know which figure is the billable one and treat the others as diagnostic rather than authoritative.

A reconciliation worth trusting matches a few things across a fixed period:
  • Reported revenue in OpenX against the remittance OpenX actually paid for the same period
  • Impression and fill counts in OpenX against the ad server's count of the same inventory
  • Any standing gap between the two, traced to a known cause such as revenue share, invalid traffic adjustments, or a timing difference, rather than left unexplained
You may want to confirm that the publisher reconciles reported revenue against the remittance on a regular cycle rather than treating the report as settled income. One approach is to match a recent paid period line by line, identify what explains the gap, and set a tolerance beyond which a discrepancy gets investigated.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, reported OpenX revenue often feeds a monthly forecast and a content-investment case, so a gap between reported and paid revenue distorts decisions about what coverage to fund. You may want to reconcile reported revenue against the remittance before the forecast is published, so the numbers driving editorial budget reflect settled income rather than an estimate.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, display revenue from OpenX sits alongside product margin in the same P&L, so an unreconciled ad-revenue figure can overstate the contribution of the content side of the business. You may want to reconcile the OpenX remittance into the same close cycle as the rest of the revenue, so the ad line is settled income rather than an estimate when the books are reviewed.

learn_more.source_title: Analytics for Prebid.js for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed revenue reporting and remittance documentation is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Match reported OpenX revenue and fill against the remittance actually paid for the same period, and look for a standing gap and whether it has a known cause.

openx_reporting.label: OpenX reporting
steps: Pull an OpenX revenue report for a recent, fully settled period, with revenue, paid impressions, and fill rate.
Pull the remittance or payment statement OpenX issued for the same period.
Match the reported revenue against the amount actually paid, and record the difference as both an absolute figure and a percentage.
Ask the publisher whether reconciliation is done on a regular cycle, and record what tolerance, if any, triggers an investigation.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: Build an ad server report covering the same OpenX inventory and period, with impressions and revenue.
Compare the ad server's impression and fill counts against OpenX's counts for the same inventory to see how far the two systems diverge.
Note whether any gap traces to a known cause, e.g., revenue share, invalid traffic adjustments, or a timezone difference, or whether it is unexplained.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the reported OpenX revenue, the remittance actually paid for the same period, and the size of any gap, along with whether reconciliation runs on a regular cycle and whether the gap has a known cause. A setup where reported revenue is reconciled against the remittance on a fixed cycle and any standing gap is explained passes. A setup where reported revenue is read as settled income with no reconciliation, or where a gap exists with no known cause, is a fail recorded with the period and the size of the discrepancy.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the reported revenue, the remittance paid, and the size of the gap above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} reads reported OpenX revenue without reconciling it against the remittance actually paid, so an estimate is being used as settled income. Reported revenue is what the auction earned before deductions, while the remittance reflects revenue share, invalid traffic adjustments, and discrepancies, so a standing gap between the two can run unnoticed and leak revenue across the whole OpenX line. {{voice_recommend}} matching reported revenue against the remittance for a recent settled period, tracing any gap to a known cause such as revenue share or an invalid traffic adjustment, and setting a tolerance beyond which a discrepancy is investigated. After that, reconcile on a regular cycle so the figure the business runs on is settled income rather than an estimate.

audit_checks.title: Report dimensions and timezones are not aligned across systems
audit_checks.impact_rationale: OpenX, a header bidding analytics adapter, and the ad server each report on their own timezone and their own set of dimensions, so a publisher comparing them can read a real gap that is only a misalignment, or miss a real gap because the boundaries do not line up. When the timezone or the dimensions differ, the reconciliation that should catch revenue problems instead produces noise, which makes the numbers harder to trust without being outright wrong.

educate.base:
When a publisher compares revenue across OpenX, a Prebid analytics adapter, and the ad server, the comparison only holds if the reports are cut the same way. Two settings decide whether they are. The first is timezone. If OpenX reports on UTC, the ad server on the publisher's local time, and the analytics adapter on a third zone, then a day in one report covers a different span of hours than a day in another, and the totals will not match even when nothing is wrong. The mismatch is largest at month boundaries, where hours of revenue land in different months depending on the zone.

The second is dimensions. A report broken out by ad unit in one system and by domain in another cannot be matched row for row, and a currency reported as USD in one place and a local currency in another will not reconcile until one is converted. Even the definition of the counted event can differ because one system may count a rendered impression while another counts a bid response, and comparing those two as if they were the same number produces a gap that is really a definition difference.

OpenX being a Prebid-compatible wrapper makes alignment more than a convenience. A publisher reading the same auction from the wrapper, the analytics adapter, and the ad server needs all three cut on a shared timezone and comparable dimensions before any discrepancy between them means something. Until then, a reconciliation cannot tell a real revenue gap from a reporting artifact.

A few signs point to dimensions and timezones that are not aligned:
  • OpenX, the analytics adapter, and the ad server each report on a different timezone, so daily and monthly totals do not cover the same hours
  • Reports are broken out by different dimensions, e.g., ad unit versus domain, so rows cannot be matched directly
  • Revenue is reported in different currencies, or the counted event differs, e.g., rendered impression versus bid response, with no conversion or mapping applied before the comparison
You may want to confirm that the reports a publisher reconciles are cut on a shared timezone and comparable dimensions before reading any gap between them as a revenue problem. One approach is to fix a single reporting timezone across the systems where it can be set, and to agree a mapping between the dimensions each system reports so the rows line up.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, monthly revenue often closes against a calendar boundary, and a timezone offset between OpenX and the ad server shifts hours of revenue across that boundary, which moves the monthly total. You may want to align the reporting timezone across the systems before the month closes, so the figure that lands in the monthly report covers the same span everywhere.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, ad revenue is often read alongside sales data that reports in the store's local timezone, so an OpenX report on a different zone will not line up with the rest of the commerce reporting. You may want to set OpenX and the analytics adapter to the same timezone the store reports on, so the ad line compares cleanly against the sales it sits next to.

learn_more.source_title: MRC, IAB Tech Lab, and MMA Revised Ad Measurement Guidelines

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's report-builder and timezone settings are documented in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Check the reporting timezone, the dimensions, the currency, and the counted event across OpenX, the analytics adapter, and the ad server to see whether the reports can be compared row for row.

openx_reporting.label: OpenX reporting
steps: Open the OpenX report settings and record the reporting timezone, the currency, and the dimensions available in the report builder.
Note whether the counted metric is a rendered impression, a paid impression, or a bid-level event, which defines what the revenue is attached to.
Build a sample report for a recent day and record its timezone, currency, and dimension breakout for comparison.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server and analytics reporting
steps: Record the reporting timezone and currency configured in the ad server and in the Prebid analytics adapter.
Compare the timezones across all three systems and note any offset that would shift revenue across a day or month boundary.
Map the dimensions each system reports, e.g., ad unit, domain, or placement, and note where they cannot be matched directly without a translation.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the reporting timezone, currency, dimensions, and counted event for OpenX, the analytics adapter, and the ad server. A setup where the timezone is shared, the currency matches or is converted, and the dimensions map cleanly so reports can be compared row for row passes. A setup where timezones differ, currencies are unconverted, or dimensions cannot be matched is a fail recorded with the specific misalignment because it makes any reconciliation between the systems unreliable.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the timezone, currency, and dimensions for each system above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} reads OpenX, the header bidding analytics adapter, and the ad server on misaligned report settings, so the numbers cannot be compared row for row. A timezone offset shifts revenue across day and month boundaries, and dimensions or currencies that do not match leave rows that cannot be reconciled, so a gap between the systems can be an artifact rather than a real revenue problem. {{voice_recommend}} fixing a single reporting timezone across the systems where it can be set, converting to a shared currency, and agreeing a mapping between the dimensions each system reports before any discrepancy is read as meaningful. After that, a comparison between the systems reflects real differences rather than reporting noise.
